title: Tokenizer mismatch
description: Integration failures when training, fine-tuning, or serving use different vocabularies, merges, or chat formatting than the checkpoint expects.
openingSummary: Tokenizer mismatch happens when the IDs fed to embeddings do not match the vocabulary the weights were trained with—silent quality loss or hard shape errors follow.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: Tokenizer mismatch covers any divergence between the tokenizer files, special-token table, chat template, and embedding matrix used at runtime versus training. Swapping a BPE merges file, resizing vocab without retraining embeddings, or skipping the chat template all qualify. Symptoms range from CUDA shape errors to subtle repetition and gibberish.

whyItMatters.title: Why It Matters
whyItMatters.body: It is one of the most common reasons a notebook demo outperforms production. Mismatch wastes GPU cycles on the wrong subword segmentation and poisons KV-cache accounting. Clear debugging starts by diffing tokenizer.json, vocab size, and template output against the model card.

simpleExample.title: Simple Example
simpleExample.body: Serving Llama weights with a GPT-2 tokenizer might still run if shapes accidentally align, but token IDs map to unrelated embeddings—outputs look fluent yet ignore the prompt.

commonConfusions.title: Common Confusions
commonConfusions.body: Tokenizer mismatch is not quantization error or wrong dtype—those affect weights, not ID mapping. It is also distinct from context-window overflow, though both show up as truncated prompts in logs.
